text: Hello my friends. I am happy you are
here.
Imagine a small boy sitting by candle
light. He is reading a book. The book is
very old. He borrowed it from a
neighbor. The neighbor lives far away.
The boy reads every night. He wants to
learn everything.
He wants to understand the world. This
boy did not go to school very much, but
he became a great man. He became a
president. He changed his country
forever.
Today we will listen to the story of
Abraham Lincoln.
Take a moment to relax and let this
story help you on your English learning
adventure.
Abraham Lincoln was born on February
He was born in Kentucky.
Kentucky is a state in America.
His home was very small. It was a log
cabin. A log cabin is a house made from
wood. The cabin had only one room.
Abraham's family was poor. His father
was a farmer. His mother was kind and
gentle.
Abraham had one sister. Her name was
Sarah.
Life was not easy for Abraham's family.
They did not have much food. They did
not have much money.
Abraham's father worked very hard on the
farm. Abraham helped his father. Even as
a small boy, Abraham worked. He carried
water. He helped with animals. He cut
wood for the fire.
The work was hard, but Abraham was
strong.
Abraham loved his mother very much. Her
name was Nancy.
Nancy told Abraham stories. She taught
him to be kind. She taught him to be
honest.
Abraham listened to everything his
mother said. He remembered her words his
whole life.
Nancy wanted Abraham to learn. She
wanted him to read.
But there were no schools nearby. There
were very few books. This made learning
difficult.
When Abraham was 7 years old, his family
moved. They moved to Indiana.
Indiana is another state in America.
They moved because his father wanted
better land.
The journey was long and hard. They
traveled through forests. They traveled
through rivers. Finally, they arrived at
their new home. Abraham's father built
another log cabin. The family started a
new life in Indiana.
Two years later, something terrible
happened. Abraham's mother became sick.
She had milk sickness.
This was a dangerous disease. There was
no medicine.
Nancy became weaker and weaker.
Abraham watched his mother suffer. He
felt very sad. He could not help her. In
Nancy died.
Abraham was only 9 years old. His heart
was broken.
He missed his mother every day. This
sadness stayed with Abraham for many
years.
After his mother died, life became
harder.
Abraham's father could not take care of
the children alone. He needed help. So
one year later, Abraham's father married
again. His new wife was named Sarah Bush
Johnston.
She was a widow. She had three children
from her first marriage.
Now Abraham had a stepmother and step
siblings.
Abraham was worried at first, but Sarah
was wonderful.
Sarah was kind to Abraham. She loved him
like her own son. She saw that Abraham
was special. She saw that he loved to
learn.
Sarah brought books to the house. She
encouraged Abraham to read. She
protected his time for learning.
Abraham was very grateful. He loved his
stepmother.
She changed his life. With Sarah's help,
Abraham could follow his dreams.
Abraham went to school sometimes, but
the schools were very simple. They were
called blab schools.
In these schools, students repeated
lessons out loud. Abraham did not go to
school for very long. Maybe he went for
one year in total. But Abraham did not
stop learning. He taught himself.
He borrowed books from neighbors. He
walked miles to get a book. He read
every book he could find.
Abraham loved books about history. He
loved books about law. He read the Bible
many times. He read a book about George
Washington.
He read a book about mathematics.
Every book taught him something new.
Abraham read slowly and carefully.
He thought about every word. He wanted
to understand everything deeply.
Sometimes he wrote things down. He wrote
on boards. He wrote on the ground with
sticks.
Paper was too expensive.
Abraham also loved to tell stories.
He remembered funny stories from books.
He told these stories to his friends and
family.
People loved to listen to Abraham.
He made them laugh. He made them think.
Abraham learned that words had power.
Good words could change how people felt.
Good words could make people understand
new ideas.
This discovery was very important for
Abraham's future.
As Abraham grew older, he became very
tall. He was more than 6 feet tall. This
was unusual at that time. Most people
were shorter. Abraham was also very
thin.
He was very strong because of farmwork,
but he looked different from other
people. Some people made jokes about how
he looked.
This hurt Abraham's feelings sometimes.
But he learned to laugh at himself.
He learned to be humble.
He learned that appearance was not
important.
Character was important.
Abraham worked many different jobs as a
young man. He split logs to make fences.
This was hard physical work.
He helped build a flatbo.
A flatbo is a simple boat for rivers.
He traveled down the Mississippi River
on this boat. He saw new places.
He saw New Orleans.
In New Orleans, Abraham saw something
that shocked him. He saw slaves being
sold.
Slaves were people who were forced to
work without freedom.
They were treated like property.
This made Abraham feel
sick inside.
He never forgot what he saw. He promised
himself that slavery was wrong. When
Abraham was 22 years old, his family
moved again. They moved to Illinois.
Illinois became Abraham's home for the
rest of his life.
Abraham started to think about his
future. He did not want to be a farmer
forever. He wanted to do something
different. He wanted to use his mind. He
wanted to help people.
Abraham decided to study law.
Law is the system of rules in a country.
Lawyers help people with legal problems.
But Abraham had no money for law school.
So he taught himself.
He borrowed law books. He read them
carefully. He studied every day. He
asked questions to lawyers he met. He
learned everything he could.
After several years of hard work,
Abraham became a lawyer. He passed the
test. Now he could work in courts. He
could help people solve problems.
Abraham was a good lawyer. He was
honest. He was fair. People trusted him.
He worked in Springfield, Illinois.
Springfield was the capital city of
Illinois.
Abraham built a reputation.
A reputation is what people think about
you. People said Abraham was a man of
strong character.
They said he always told the truth. They
said he worked hard for justice.
These qualities made Abraham successful.
Abraham also became interested in
politics.
Politics is about government and
leadership.
Abraham believed he could help his
community.
He believed he could make life better
for people.
So he decided to run for office. He
wanted to become a representative.
A representative is someone who speaks
for the people.
Abraham gave speeches. He talked to
voters. He explained his ideas.
People liked Abraham. They liked his
honesty.
They liked his simple way of speaking.
Abraham was elected to the Illinois
State Legislature.
He was only 25 years old.
Around this time, Abraham met a woman
named Mary Todd.
Mary was from a wealthy family. She was
educated.
She was interested in politics.
Abraham and Mary talked for many hours.
They shared ideas.
They fell in love.
But their relationship was not easy.
They had different backgrounds.
Mary's family did not approve at first.
Abraham and Mary separated for a while,
but their love was strong.
they got married. They built a life
together in Springfield.
Abraham and Mary had four sons.
Their names were Robert, Edward,
William, and Thomas.
Abraham loved his children very much. He
played with them. He told them stories.
He was a gentle father.
But tragedy came to their family. Edward
died when he was only 3 years old.
Later, William died when he was 11.
These losses broke Abraham's heart. He
and Mary suffered greatly,
but they continued forward.
They held on to each other.
Abraham continued his work in politics.
He served in the United States Congress
for one term.
Congress is the national government.
Abraham spoke against slavery.
He believed slavery was morally wrong.
He believed all people should be free.
But many people disagreed with him.
Slavery was an important part of the
economy in the south.
Southern states had large farms called
plantations.
These plantations used slave labor.
The country was divided on this issue.
Abraham ran for the United States
Senate. The Senate is part of Congress.
He ran against a man named Steven
Douglas.
Douglas was famous.
He was a powerful politician.
Abraham and Douglas had seven debates.
A debate is a formal argument.
People from all over came to listen.
Abraham spoke clearly about slavery.
He said slavery was wrong.
He said it should not spread to new
states.
Douglas had different views. The debates
made Abraham famous across the country.
Abraham lost  the
election.
But people remembered his words. They
remembered his principles.
In 1860, something amazing happened.
Abraham was chosen to run for president.
President is the leader of the United
States. This was a great honor, but it
was also a great challenge. The country
was in trouble. The North and South
disagreed strongly about slavery. Some
southern states said they would leave
the Union if Abraham won. The Union
means the United States as one country.
The situation was dangerous.
Abraham ran his campaign with dignity.
He spoke about keeping the country
together. He spoke about freedom and
equality.
He reminded people that all men are
created equal. This idea comes from
America's founding documents. Abraham
believed in this idea deeply.
On November 6th, 1860, Abraham Lincoln
was elected president. He was 51 years
old. He had come from a small log cabin
to the highest office in the land.
But before Abraham could even start his
work as president, the southern states
began to leave. They formed their own
country called the Confederate States.
They wanted to keep slavery. They did
not accept Abraham as their president.
This was the beginning of the civil war.
A civil war is when people in the same
country fight each other. It is the
saddest kind of war. Brothers fought
against brothers. Friends fought against
friends.
The country was torn apart.
Abraham became president in March 1861.
One month later, the war began. Abraham
had to make very difficult decisions. He
had to lead the country through its
darkest time.
The war lasted four years. More than
600,000 people died. This was more than
any other American war. Abraham carried
this weight every day. He could not
sleep well. His face showed his sadness,
but he never gave up.
He believed in the Union. He believed in
freedom.
Abraham did something historic. He
issued the Emancipation Proclamation.
This document declared that slaves in
the Confederate States were free. It was
a bold move. It changed the meaning of
the war. Now the war was not just about
keeping the Union together.
It was also about ending slavery. It was
about human freedom and dignity.
This proclamation did not free all
slaves immediately, but it was an
important step. It showed the world what
America stood for.
Abraham also gave a famous speech in
November 1863.
The speech was at Gettysburg,
Pennsylvania.
Gettysburg was the location of a
terrible battle. Thousands of soldiers
had died there. Abraham spoke for only 2
minutes. His speech was very short, but
his words were powerful.
He talked about equality.
He talked about democracy.
He talked about honoring those who died
by continuing their work. He said, "The
nation must have a new birth of
freedom."
People still remember this speech today.
It is called the Gettysburg Address.
The war finally ended in April 1865.
The Union won. The country would stay
together. Slavery would end completely.
The 13th amendment to the Constitution
abolished slavery forever. Abraham had
led the country through its greatest
crisis.
He had preserved the Union.
He had helped millions of people find
freedom.
Abraham was tired. He had aged greatly.
But he was hopeful. He wanted to heal
the country. He wanted North and South
to come together again in peace.
But then tragedy  struck.
On April 14th, 1865,
Abraham went to a theater. He went to
watch a play with his wife Mary. The
theater was in Washington, D.C.
During the play, a man named John Wils
Booth entered Abraham's box.
Booth supported the Confederacy.
He was angry that the South had lost. He
shot Abraham in the head. Abraham was
badly hurt. Doctors tried to help him,
but Abraham died the next morning. He
was 56 years old.
The nation was in shock. People
everywhere cried. They had lost their
leader.
They had lost a man who represented hope
and freedom. Abraham Lincoln's story is
one of the greatest in history. He was
born with nothing. He grew up in
poverty. He had almost no formal
education.
But he never stopped learning. He never
stopped trying to improve himself. He
believed in hard work. He believed in
honesty. He believed in treating all
people with respect and dignity.
These beliefs guided his entire life.
Abraham faced many challenges. He lost
his mother when he was young. He lost
two of his children. He led his country
through a terrible war. He saw so much
suffering and death. But he never became
bitter. He never gave up. He stayed
focused on what was right. He stayed
focused on justice and freedom.
This is why people remember him. This is
why he is called one of America's
greatest presidents.
Abraham Lincoln showed us that our
beginning does not determine our ending.
He showed us that one person can make a
difference. He showed us that courage
and character matter more than wealth or
status. His life teaches us to keep
learning, to stay honest, and to fight
for what is right, even when it is
difficult.
These lessons are still important today.
They will always be important. When we
listen to stories like Abraham Lincoln's
story, we learn more than history. We
learn about human strength. We learn
about hope. We learn that change is
possible. And as we listen to these
stories in English, we are also on our
own journey. We are learning a new
language. We are opening doors to new
understanding.
Every story helps us grow. Every story
helps us improve.
Thank you for listening today, my
friends. I hope Abraham Lincoln's story
touched your heart. I hope it inspired
you to keep learning and growing. If you
enjoyed this story, please take a moment
to subscribe. A simple subscription
helps us create more stories for you. It
helps us continue this journey of
learning English together through
peaceful, meaningful storytelling.
You can also leave a comment below. Tell
us what you learned from Abraham
Lincoln's life. Tell us which part of
his story moved you the most. And if you
found this story helpful, please click
the like button. Your support means
everything. Remember, learning English
is a journey. It takes time. It takes
patience.
But every story you listen to makes you
stronger. Every word you understand
brings you closer to your goals. Keep
listening. Keep learning. Keep believing
in yourself.
Just like Abraham Lincoln, you can
achieve great things.
Until our next story, take care of
yourself, be kind to others, and keep
walking forward on your learning
adventure. I will see you soon with
another story. Goodbye for now, my
